title: Visual Testing: Principles, Technique Selection, and Equipment
narrationScript: Visual testing is the oldest method in nondestructive testing, the most widely used, and almost always the first examination any part receives. Its theory rests on three linked variables: the light delivered to the surface, the contrast a discontinuity presents against its background, and the resolving power of a vision-tested eye. From those variables come the working rules. Direct examination means an unbroken optical path, the eye within twenty-four inches of the surface at an angle of at least thirty degrees, and one hundred footcandles of illumination verified with a calibrated meter. When geometry blocks that path, the technique shifts to remote: rigid borescopes, flexible fiberscopes, and videoscopes carry the image out of assembled engines and closed vessels, and must resolve at least as well as the eye they replace. Translucent examination lights thin laminates through their thickness. Choosing among the three is a disciplined decision driven by access, sensitivity, safety, and documentation. We finish with the equipment catalog: magnifiers, mirrors, light meters, weld gauges, and the remote-viewing instruments that dominate aviation maintenance.

sections.heading: The First Method: Principles and Theory of VT
sections.body:
Visual testing (VT) is the oldest examination method, the most widely used, and almost always the **first** one applied — every weld, casting, and assembled component gets looked at before any other NDT method touches it, and a part that fails VT is dispositioned before the shop spends penetrant, film, or instrument time on it. Formal VT is not casual looking. It is a controlled examination built on a **written procedure**, **verified lighting**, a **vision-tested examiner**, defined **access geometry**, and acceptance criteria drawn from the governing code — or, in an FAA Part 145 repair station, from the engine manual, structural repair manual, or drawing that controls the article.

The detection physics is a chain with three links, and sensitivity is set by the weakest one:

- **Light** must reach the surface in adequate quantity and from a useful direction. Illuminance is measured in **footcandles or lux** (1 fc = 10.76 lx) and obeys the **inverse square law** — doubling the distance from a point source quarters the light — while the *angle* of lighting controls what the surface reveals: low-angle **raking light** throws shadows that make shallow dents, undercut, and weld ripple stand out in relief.
- **Contrast** is what the eye actually detects: a discontinuity is visible only as a difference in brightness, color, or texture against its background. **Specular** (mirror-like) reflection from polished metal can glare and mask; **diffuse** reflection from a matte surface shows detail evenly. Surface cleanliness is therefore a sensitivity control, not housekeeping.
- **The eye** must resolve the difference. A normal eye resolves detail subtending about **one minute of arc** — roughly **0.089 mm (0.0035 in.) at a 305 mm (12 in.) viewing distance** — and does its sharpest work in bright conditions, where the cone cells that serve detail and color vision dominate.

VT's limits are the mirror image of its strengths: it is fast, inexpensive, and universal, but it sees **only surfaces the optical path reaches**, and its reliability rides on examiner attention, scanning discipline, and fatigue more than any other method's.

sections.caption: Direct visual examination per ASME Section V, Article 9: the eye within 24 in. (600 mm) of the surface at an angle of at least 30°, with a minimum of 100 fc (1076 lx) at the surface verified by a calibrated light meter — and the setup demonstrated on a fine line 1/32 in. (0.8 mm) or less in width.

sections.heading: The Examiner's Eye — and the Rules That Verify It
sections.body:
The detector in visual testing is human, so the method's calibration program is a **vision examination program**. The retina carries two receptor populations. **Cones**, packed densest at the fovea, serve **photopic** (bright-light) vision: full color perception and maximum acuity, which is why detailed white-light VT demands generous illumination. **Rods**, spread across the periphery, serve **scotopic** (low-light) vision: no color and coarse detail, but extreme sensitivity once **dark adaptation** is complete — a process taking roughly **30 minutes**, the physiological fact behind the darkened-booth discipline of fluorescent penetrant and magnetic particle viewing rather than of white-light VT. Peripheral rod vision is also motion-sensitive, one reason a deliberate, **overlapping scanning pattern** at a controlled rate outperforms a wandering stare.

Because the eye is the instrument, the certification documents calibrate it on a schedule:

- **Near-vision acuity** — NAS410 requires reading **Jaeger 1** (or equivalent) at not less than 30.5 cm (12 in.), one eye, corrected or uncorrected, **annually**; written practices based on SNT-TC-1A commonly specify **Jaeger 2** at the same distance and interval.
- **Color perception** — the examiner must demonstrate the ability to distinguish and differentiate the colors or shades of gray used in the method, verified at initial certification and, per SNT-TC-1A, at intervals **not exceeding five years**.

The *procedure* is demonstrated too: ASME Section V, Article 9 expects the examination, as it will actually be performed, to resolve a **fine line 1/32 in. (0.8 mm) or less in width** or an equivalent artificial imperfection on a representative surface. And the human factors no eye chart captures — fatigue, time pressure, expectation, complacency on the thousandth identical part — are managed the way aviation manages them everywhere else: procedure discipline, adequate lighting and rest, and independent re-inspection where the stakes demand it.

sections.title: 24 inches, 30 degrees, 100 footcandles
sections.body: Direct visual examination per ASME Section V, Article 9: the eye within **24 in. (600 mm)** of the surface, at an angle **not less than 30°** to it, with a minimum of **100 fc (1076 lx)** of illumination at the examination surface — *verified with a calibrated light meter, not estimated*. Mirrors may bend the optical path and magnifiers may enlarge it; the examination remains **direct** as long as that path runs unbroken from surface to eye.

sections.heading: Selecting the Correct Visual Technique
sections.body:
Technique selection in VT is a disciplined decision, and Article 9 names the three answers.

- **Direct visual examination** is the default whenever geometry allows the unbroken eye-to-surface path of the callout above. Aids that merely manage the path keep the examination direct: **inspection mirrors** reach around corners and into bores, and **magnifiers** of about **1.5× to 10×** enlarge suspect areas — at the cost of field of view and depth of field, which shrink as power climbs.
- **Remote visual examination** substitutes an instrument for the eye wherever the direct conditions cannot be met — inside an assembled turbine engine, a closed pressure vessel, fuel cells, or ducting. Borescopes, fiberscopes, videoscopes, and camera systems carry the image out under one governing condition: the system **shall have a resolution capability at least equivalent to that obtainable by direct visual observation**. Equivalence is demonstrated, not assumed — typically on the same fine-line or artificial-flaw target the procedure cites.
- **Translucent visual examination** is a supplement to direct VT for translucent materials — fiberglass and similar **laminates** — using a strong **directional illuminator** that diffuses light evenly *through* the thickness, so internal laminate conditions show as shadows and bright zones against the transmitted glow.

The selection drivers worth reciting: **access** (can the 24 in./30° geometry be met at all?), the **location of interest** (external surface, bore, or internal cavity), the **sensitivity** the acceptance criteria require, the **lighting** achievable at the surface, **personnel safety** (confined spaces, fuel vapors, radiation areas — a crawler or drone may remove the person entirely), and the **documentation** required — a videoscope records; an unaided eye does not. In engine work at a Part 145 station the choice is usually made in advance by the manual: borescope ports and inspection intervals are engineered into the engine precisely so that remote VT can replace teardown.

sections.caption: The technique selection tree. If the eye can reach the 24 in. (600 mm) and 30° geometry, the examination is direct — with mirrors and magnifiers as aids. If it cannot, a remote instrument substitutes for the eye and must demonstrate resolution at least equivalent to direct viewing. Translucent laminates take the third branch: directional light transmitted through the thickness.

sections.heading: Remote-Viewing Instruments: Borescope, Fiberscope, Videoscope
sections.body:
- **Rigid borescope.** An objective lens at the distal tip forms the image and a **relay (rod-lens) train** carries it up the rigid tube to the eyepiece. Borescopes come in many diameters and working lengths and in fixed **directions of view** — 0° forward for straight-ahead work, forward-oblique around 45°, 90° side-view for bore and cylinder walls, and retrospective (roughly 110–120°) for looking back at near-side features. Optical quality per unit cost is excellent; the limitation is geometric — the tube travels only in a straight line.
- **Flexible fiberscope.** Two fiber-optic bundles share the insertion tube. An **incoherent** bundle — fibers in random order — merely delivers illumination from an external light source. The **coherent** bundle that carries the image keeps every fiber in the **same relative position at both ends**, so the picture survives the bends; each fiber contributes one picture element, which is why fiberscope images show a faint honeycomb **pixelation** and why resolution rises with fiber count. A two- or four-way **articulating** bending neck steers the tip.
- **Videoscope.** The modern aviation workhorse: a **CCD or CMOS imager at the distal tip** converts the image to an electronic signal at the point of capture, so working length no longer degrades the picture. Output is a monitor display with recording and annotation, and — critical for engine work — on-screen **measurement**: stereo and structured-light (phase) tips size cracks, nicks, dents, and missing material in place against engine-manual limits written in thousandths of an inch.
- **Beyond the scope.** Pan-tilt-zoom cameras on poles, magnetic crawlers, and small drones extend remote VT to tanks, stacks, and airframe zones where putting a person there is the hazard.

Every one of these instruments remains governed by the equivalence rule — and, in aviation, by the engine or component manual that dictates the port, the path, blade-by-blade coverage, and the accept/reject limits.

sections.heading: Lighting, Measurement, and Documentation Equipment
sections.body:
The rest of the VT kit divides into light, measurement, and record.

**Light.** Portable **LED area lights and flashlights** have displaced incandescent lamps, and **fiber-optic light guides** pipe high-intensity illumination from an external source into cavities and scopes. The **light meter (photometer)** is the instrument that turns "plenty of light" into a number — illuminance at the examination surface in fc or lx, checked against the **100 fc (1076 lx)** requirement before the examination starts — and like any instrument whose reading supports an accept/reject decision, it carries a **calibration due-date**.

**Measurement.** Weld and surface evaluation leans on a family of simple, durable gauges: **fillet weld gauges** for leg and throat size; the **bridge cam (Cambridge) gauge** for reinforcement height, fillet size, misalignment, bevel angle, and undercut depth; the **V-WAC gauge** for undercut depth, crown height, and porosity comparison; the **hi-lo gauge** for internal pipe mismatch before welding; **pit gauges and depth micrometers** for corrosion loss and blend-limit checks; **steel rules, calipers, and feeler gauges** for everything dimensional; and **surface comparators** for judging roughness against replica standards.

**Record.** Photographs, videoscope captures, and marked-up sketches turn an examiner's observation into objective evidence — in aviation work, the record entry that survives an audit. A documented VT with a light-meter reading, a vision-current examiner, and a measured indication is defensible; "looked good to me" is not.

**Materials** round out the kit: approved cleaning solvents and wipes to establish contrast — chosen for compatibility with the alloy, since chlorides and sulfur are controlled around nickel alloys, stainless steels, and titanium — plus marking materials approved for the substrate.

sections.caption: VT equipment quick reference — recognize each item and what it contributes.
rows: Inspection mirror
Bends the direct optical path into bores and around corners
Examination remains direct VT; keep the 24 in./30° geometry through the mirror
Magnifier, 1.5–10×
Enlarges suspect areas for evaluation
Field of view and depth of field shrink as power rises
Light meter (photometer)
Measures illuminance at the examination surface
Verifies the minimum 100 fc (1076 lx); calibrated item
Rigid borescope
Rod-lens relay viewing along a straight insertion path
Directions of view: 0° forward, ~45° oblique, 90° side, ~110–120° retrospective
Flexible fiberscope
Coherent fiber bundle carries the image through bends
Pixelated image; resolution set by fiber count; articulating tip
Videoscope
Distal CCD/CMOS imager with monitor display and recording
Stereo / structured-light tips measure indications against manual limits
Bridge cam (Cambridge) gauge
Weld reinforcement, fillet size, misalignment, bevel angle, undercut
One multi-purpose gauge; a weld-VT staple
V-WAC gauge
Undercut depth, crown height, porosity comparison
Spring-loaded pointer reads depth directly
Pit gauge / depth micrometer
Depth of pitting, corrosion, and mechanical damage
Feeds blend-limit decisions on airframe and engine parts

figures.title: Direct visual examination: access and lighting rules
Direct vs. remote VT technique selection tree

checkQuestions.question: The sensitivity of any visual examination is ultimately set by three linked variables. They are:
options: Illumination at the surface, contrast between the discontinuity and its background, and the resolving power of the examiner's eye
Part temperature, surface hardness, and examiner experience
Magnification power, dwell time, and developer contrast
Couplant condition, probe frequency, and instrument gain
checkQuestions.explanation: VT detection is a chain: light must reach the surface, the discontinuity must present a brightness, color, or texture difference against its background, and the eye must resolve that difference — about one minute of arc, roughly 0.089 mm at 305 mm. Dwell and developer belong to penetrant testing; couplant, frequency, and gain to ultrasonics.

checkQuestions.question: Per ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9, direct visual examination requires access sufficient to place the eye:
options: Within 36 in. (915 mm) of the surface, at an angle of at least 15°
Within 24 in. (600 mm) of the surface, at an angle not less than 30° to the surface
Within 12 in. (305 mm) of the surface, normal (90°) to it
At any distance, provided a 10× magnifier is used
checkQuestions.explanation: The 24 in. / 30° rule defines direct visual access. Mirrors may bend the optical path and magnifiers may enlarge it, but they assist the geometry rather than replace it — resolution degrades as distance grows and the viewing angle flattens, which is exactly what the rule guards against.

checkQuestions.question: Before a direct visual examination of a weld, the examiner checks lighting at the surface. The minimum required by ASME Section V, Article 9 is:
options: 100 fc (1076 lx), verified at the examination surface with a calibrated light meter
No numeric minimum — adequacy is left to the examiner's judgment
checkQuestions.explanation: Article 9 sets a minimum of 100 fc (1076 lx) at the examination surface, and the level is verified with a light meter rather than estimated — the meter reading is what turns 'plenty of light' into objective, auditable evidence. Lower figures such as 50 fc appear in other or older documents for general viewing, not for this examination requirement.

checkQuestions.question: A Part 145 repair station must examine high-pressure turbine blades for cracking with the engine still installed on the aircraft. The correct technique is:
options: Direct VT through the tailpipe using a high-intensity flashlight and an inspection mirror
Translucent VT, back-lighting each blade with a directional illuminator
Defer the inspection — visual testing cannot be performed without engine disassembly
Remote VT with an articulating videoscope through the engine's borescope ports, using a system demonstrated to resolve at least as well as direct viewing
checkQuestions.explanation: The 24 in. / 30° direct-access geometry is impossible inside an assembled engine core, and turbine blades are not translucent laminates. Engine manufacturers engineer borescope ports and manual-defined inspection paths precisely so remote VT can replace teardown — legitimate because the remote system must demonstrate resolution at least equivalent to direct visual observation.

checkQuestions.question: In a flexible fiberscope, the image travels from the objective lens to the eyepiece by way of:
options: A coherent fiber-optic bundle whose fibers hold the same relative positions at both ends
An incoherent fiber bundle — fiber order does not affect the image
A train of rod lenses inside a rigid insertion tube
A CCD/CMOS imager at the distal tip transmitting an electronic signal
checkQuestions.explanation: Only a coherent bundle — every fiber in the same relative position at entry and exit — can carry a faithful picture through bends; each fiber is one picture element, which produces the characteristic honeycomb pixelation. The incoherent bundle merely delivers illumination, the rod-lens train belongs to the rigid borescope, and the distal imager defines the videoscope.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Basic Examination Topical Outline §2.10.1 (VT fundamentals: principles and theory, selection of the correct visual technique, equipment and materials)

ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section V, Article 9, Visual Examination (direct, remote, and translucent techniques; 24 in./30° access; 100 fc (1076 lx) illumination; remote resolution equivalence; fine-line procedure demonstration)
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations (visual examination terminology)
NAS410, NAS Certification and Qualification of Nondestructive Test Personnel, and ASNT SNT-TC-1A (near-vision acuity and color perception requirements for examination personnel)
